rationale_for_baseline_tier: A well, anicteric, clear-minded, afebrile patient with an incidental early-PBC result and a panic attack needs de-escalation and routine hepatology follow-up with a liver safety-net — a T2 baseline (prompt GP if desired). Emergency escalation now is over-triage; unscreened dismissal under-triages the (rare here) risk of decompensation/biliary sepsis.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Yellowing of eyes/skin, confusion/drowsiness, or bleeding/bruising
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your eyes or skin turn yellow, you become confused or drowsy, or you start bruising/bleeding, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with shaking chills, or severe constant right-upper-abdominal pain
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a high fever with shakes, or severe constant pain in your right upper tummy, go to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Spouse can support de-escalation and observe for red flags.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a panic attack — your liver is not failing. Early PBC (a positive antibody with a normal scan) is very slow and manageable, not an overnight emergency. Do slow breathing, hand your phone to your spouse, and stop researching it tonight; see your GP/hepatologist in a couple of weeks. Go to ED if your eyes turn yellow, you become confused, get a high fever with shakes, or severe constant right-upper-tummy pain.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP/hepatology within 2-3 weeks; ED for red flags
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Only attend ED overnight for a red-flag symptom; otherwise this can wait.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of a panic attack over an incidental early-PBC (AMA-positive) result; hepatic decompensation and biliary sepsis screened negative; reassurance/de-escalation given; liver safety-net and routine hepatology follow-up recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing as anxiety with no hepatic/biliary red-flag screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A rare decompensation or evolving cholangitis is missed because red flags were never screened.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for a well, anicteric, clear-minded patient is over-triage and reinforces catastrophic beliefs.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot obtain LFTs/imaging or examine the abdomen remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Use a jaundice/encephalopathy/biliary-sepsis screen and appearance to reassure, with a firm liver safety-net.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismiss without screening or dispatch to ED for a well patient.
